summary:
The Treaty of Paris (February 10, 1763 AD) ended the [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]] and produced the most significant redistribution of colonial territories in 18th-century history, establishing British imperial dominance across North America, the Caribbean, and India, and setting the conditions for both the [[event:106|American Revolution]] and the [[event:107|French Revolution]]. The treaty was signed between Britain, France, and Spain (Portugal also participated); Austria and Prussia separately concluded the Treaty of Hubertusburg ([[event:3292|1763]]) settling the European continental dimension.

Britain's gains were extraordinary: from France, Canada and all French territories east of the Mississippi River (giving Britain control of all of North America east of the Mississippi), several Caribbean islands (Grenada, St. Vincent, Dominica, Tobago), Senegal in West Africa, and recognition of British supremacy in India; from Spain (which had entered the war in 1761 as a French ally and been defeated), Florida (both East and West). France retained only New Orleans (briefly — it was secretly ceded to Spain in 1762 via the Treaty of Fontainebleau), several Caribbean islands (Martinique, Guadeloupe, Saint-Domingue), and fishing rights off Newfoundland. Spain received Louisiana (west of the Mississippi) from France as compensation for Florida. The settlement left France deeply humiliated and resentful: the costs of the war, combined with the loss of its American empire, contributed directly to the fiscal crisis that triggered the 1789 Revolution. For Britain, the victory was transformative but created a new problem: how to govern and tax a vastly expanded empire, particularly the unruly American colonies — leading within 12 years to the events that would strip Britain of its most important colonial possession.
